text: Hello friends! This is a case of da Vinci robotic Roux-en-Y hepatico-jejunctomy. This patient has colicectomy done approximately one year ago and due to the collateral damage or remote injury there was CVD structure. Three times this patient has gone with the stent and even metal stent was tried but it didn't cure and ultimately it was decided that robotic hepatico-jejunctomy has to be performed. and unfortunately a structure is very high it is just near the hilum so we have to do and with the davinci robot so that will give the proper access and better suturing and better manipulation so as you know that this is four arm davinci robot and as you know that davinci robot itself cannot do the access Axis means entry inside the abdomen with the telescope and instrument that you have to do. And generally the axis principles of the robotic surgery is same as laparoscopic surgery. So here we are using variational technique to do the axis. And as you know that for the variational technique we will take two ellipse forceps. And this ellipse forceps you can apply just to retract the crease of the umbilicus.

The assistant retracts the umbilicus using forceps, while a small stab wound is made over the inferior crease with an 11 blade. A 12mm stereoscope is positioned for binocular vision. The surgeon holds the varus needle like a dart, performing irrigation, suction, and hanging drop tests to confirm entry into the negative pressure cavity. Tubing is attached to the insufflator, and insufflation begins at a rate of one liter per minute. The surgeon monitors for proper insufflation and prepares to insert additional trocars for the robotic surgery.

The surgeon enlarges the initial incision using an 11-number knife, then introduces a 12-millimeter trocar into the wound with a screwing motion, holding it like a pistol. The trocar is positioned perpendicular to the surface, allowing for the insertion of the telescope for docking.

The da Vinci robotic system is docked, and the surgeon begins the procedure using the master controller to manipulate instruments inside the abdomen. The viewport provides a three-dimensional view of the surgical field. The surgeon addresses adhesions between the duodenum and the right lobe of the liver, as well as between the liver and the anterior abdominal wall, using a harmonic scalpel to carefully separate the flimsy adhesions and coagulate the inferior border of the liver to prevent oozing.

The surgeon carefully removes omental adhesions to allow traction on the liver, aiming to improve visibility of the common bile duct (CBD). The duodenum is adhered near the gallbladder fossa, requiring selective adhesiolysis. The common hepatic duct is visible, slightly dilated, but short in length, with surrounding adhesions being dissected to prepare for the hepatico-jejunostomy.

The surgeon performs adhesiolysis around the hepatic duct, revealing the blue-colored portal vein below. An atraumatic grasper and intestinal clamp are used for blunt dissection, gently separating the tissue due to the absence of tactile feedback in the da Vinci robot. The hepatic duct is mobilized, and the area is cleared of adhesions, preparing for side-to-side anastomosis with the jejunum loop. The ERCP stent is visible and will be removed during the procedure.

The surgeon punctures the anterior surface of the hepatic duct below the hilum using a harmonic scalpel, ensuring the posterior wall remains uninjured. Bile is visibly released as the incision is enlarged. A stent, previously introduced via ERCP, is visible and removed using a Da Vinci robotic grasper. The stent, initially stained yellow by bile, appears white upon removal. The lumen is clear, with no stones present. The incision is further enlarged using cold scissors, with the assistant cutting and the left hand dilating. A gauze piece is placed over the area to apply pressure and stop any oozing from the margins.

The surgeon prepares a Roux limb for the Roux-en-Y hepatico-jejunostomy by lifting the ligamentum teres and taking a jejunum loop. The thin patient allows easy manipulation of the jejunum, which is pulled to reach the hilum. A magentric window is created using harmonic or ligature, allowing passage of the endo GI linear stapler to divide the jejunum and form the limbs. The white cartridge of the stapler is fired, and the jejunum is cut. The surgeon then performs an ileo-ileostomy and side-to-side anastomosis by perforating the anti-magentric border, ensuring the Roux limb is ready for the hepatico-jejunostomy.

The surgeon performs a side-to-side anastomosis between the jejunum and hepatic duct using an endo-GI linear stapler. An 8mm incision is made on the anti-mesenteric border of the jejunum to introduce the stapler. The jejunal lumen is opened and aligned parallel to the hepatic duct lumen, and the stapler is fired to complete the anastomosis. The opening created by the stapler is closed using a Stratafix or V-lock suture, ensuring the posterior valve is not included in the suture bites.

The surgeon performs continuous suturing using a barb suture in a Roux-en-Y procedure, employing an out-to-in and in-to-out technique to close the internal layer. The mucosa is buried during the process, and the suture is tied carefully to avoid cut-through, utilizing visual interpretation due to the lack of tactile feedback in robotic surgery.

The surgeon completes the closure of the enterotomy, ensuring the termination occurs on healthy tissue with careful approximation of the edges. The robotic system's wrist articulation allows for precise suturing, and the final termination is achieved using a square knot. The enterotomy closure is visually confirmed without any openings, as no pressure tests can be applied in this low-pressure zone.

The surgeon prepares the Roux limb for hepatico-jejunostomy by perforating the anti-mesenteric border of the jejunum with a 15mm incision. The lumen is clearly visible, and the anastomosis begins with laparoscopic suturing. The surgeon uses a surgeon's knot with two wraps followed by alternating wraps to approximate the edges. An assistant holds the tissue while another assistant uses scissors to cut the suture. The surgeon takes a reverse bite in the jejunum and the common hepatic duct, demonstrating the ease of articulation with robotic instruments.

The surgeon introduces a new suture to continue suturing the left margin of the common hepatic duct wound. The suture is taken from out to in, ensuring a full-thickness bite that does not cut through the tissue or stray too far from the margin. The left margin is approximated using an intracorporeal surgeon's knot, achieving a secure and visually satisfactory closure.

The surgeon completes the final suture on the superior margin of the hepatico-jejunostomy, using the Da Vinci robot for precise approximation of the common hepatic duct and jejunum. The assistant releases tension by pulling the jejunum upward, facilitating the approximation. After removing the needle, a drain is placed, and all sponges are removed from the surgical field.
